request.question: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?

title: Björk
Where Is the Line
Dancer in the Dark
List of songs recorded by Björk
I've Seen It All
Jófríður Ákadóttir
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir
Innocence (Björk song)
Michael Flessas
Selmasongs
sentences: Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.

Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.
She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.

Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.
"Where Is the Line" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer Björk for her sixth studio album "Medúlla".
It was written by the singer herself and co-produced with longtime collaborator Mark Bell.

The track was originally intended to be released as the album's third commercial single, but its release was cancelled just before Björk released the "Drawing Restraint 9" soundtrack, in order to focus all promotional efforts on the film and soundtrack instead.
Some promotional white labels vinyls were released around 2005.
A music video for the song was made by Icelandic Artist Gabríela Friðriksdóttir.
Upon the cancellation of the single, the video was used as part of a video installation project by Friðriksdóttir.
Dancer in the Dark (Danish: Danser i mørket) is a 2000 Danish musical drama film directed by Lars von Trier.

It stars Icelandic singer Björk as a daydreaming immigrant factory worker who suffers from a degenerative eye condition and is saving up to pay for an operation to prevent her young son from suffering the same fate.
Catherine Deneuve, David Morse, Cara Seymour, Peter Stormare, Siobhan Fallon Hogan, and Joel Grey also star.
Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.
She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.
She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.
Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.
"I've Seen It All" is a song by Icelandic singer Björk, with lyrics by Sjón and Lars von Trier.
It was released as the first promotional single from the "Dancer in the Dark" soundtrack, "".
The song features vocals from Radiohead frontman Thom Yorke.
It was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Lyrically, it speaks of one coming to terms with the fact that they are going blind.
Jófríður Ákadóttir, known professionally as JFDR, is an Icelandic singer, songwriter and multi-instrumentalist born in 1994.
She is known for her solo project JFDR as well as her work with Samaris, Pascal Pinon and Gangly and works closely with producer and multi-instrumentalist .
Her father is Icelandic composer and musician Áki Ásgeirsson.
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir (] ; born 11 November 1986), known outside Iceland simply as Greta Salóme (] ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, and violinist in the Icelandic Symphony Orchestra.
She represented Iceland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2012 in Baku, Azerbaijan, in a duet with singer Jónsi with the song "Never Forget".
They qualified from the first semi-final into the Eurovision final and eventually finished in 20th place with 46 points.
She represented Iceland again, this time as a soloist, in the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 with the song "Hear Them Calling", where she failed to qualify.
"Innocence" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.
The song was released as the second single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta".
It is co-produced by record producer Timbaland and his protégé Danja.
The song was premiered on Björk's MySpace profile on March 19, 2007 as part of the fan contest to submit ideas for the music video.
The single was released exclusively as a digital download on July 23, 2007.
Michael C. Flessas (born June 2, 1959 in Miami, Florida), is the birth name of American actor Michael Flessas, who is of Greek ancestry.
Flessas' most notable film role was "Angry Man" in the Cannes Film Festival 2000 Palme d'Or winning film "Dancer in the Dark" directed by Danish film director Lars von Trier.
Originally, the director himself considered playing the role but, instead, the role was given to Flessas.
"Dancer in the Dark" starred Icelandic singer/actress Björk who won the Best Actress award at Cannes for her role.

French film icon, César Award winner, and Academy Award nominee Catherine Deneuve, and other noteworthy artists such as Academy Award and Tony Award winner Joel Grey, Peter Stormare, David Morse, and Stellan Skarsgård also performed in the multiple prize winning film.
One of Björk's songs for the film received an Academy Award nomination for Best Song.
Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.
It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".
In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.
The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?
hop1_docs: Mary J. Blige | Mary Jane Blige ( ; born January 11, 1971) is an American singer, songwriter, rapper, model, record producer and actress.  Starting her career as a backing singer on Uptown Records in 1989, Blige released her first album, "What's the 411? ", in 1992, and has released 13 studio albums since and made over 150 guest appearances on other albums and soundtracks.

Selmasongs | Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.  It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".  In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.  The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure | Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure is a soundtrack album, released on June 23, 2004, for the anime series "Samurai Champloo".  It is the second of four soundtrack albums released for the show.  The album was produced by Japanese DJ/producer Nujabes, and American MC/producer Fat Jon, and featured Japanese rapper Shing02 and Japanese singer-songwriter MINMI on the album's first and last tracks, respectively.
List of awards and nominations received by Anahí | Anahí is a Mexican singer-songwriter, actress, record producer and dancer.

Vespertine | Vespertine is the fourth solo album by Icelandic musician Björk, released on 27 August 2001, on One Little Indian Records.  It was recorded at locations in Spain, Iceland, and the United States in 2000; production began during the filming of "Dancer in the Dark", which was characterized by conflict between the singer and director Lars von Trier. " Björk", a self-titled coffee table book containing photographs of the singer throughout her career, was released simultaneously with the album.  "Vespertine"' s sound reflected Björk's newly found interest in the music of artists such as Opiate and Console, who were also enlisted as producers.
Charlotte Devaney | Charlotte Devaney is a British DJ, producer, singer, dancer and actress.

Howard Donald | Howard Paul Donald (born 28 April 1968), is an English singer, songwriter, drummer, pianist, dancer, DJ and record producer.  He is a member of English pop-group Take That and a judge on the German reality talent show "Got to Dance".
hop1_titles: List of awards and nominations received by Anahí

- Vespertine release date/context — [5] "27 August 2001"

- None among named candidates in the passages; "Björk" is the sole Icelandic musician named.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Passages do not explicitly state Björk's occupations as "singer", "songwriter", "record producer", or "DJ" (they show "musician" and that she "starred" as an actress).

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page "Björk - Wikipedia" to confirm full listed occupations (singer, songwriter, record producer, DJ).
- Fetch page "Selmasongs - Wikipedia" (already cited) to confirm soundtrack authorship context.
- Search query: "Björk singer songwriter record producer DJ occupations" 
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.
response.hop2_query: Björk singer songwriter record producer DJ
hop2_docs: Björk | Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.  Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.  She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.  Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.

When Björk Met Attenborough | When Björk Met Attenborough is a 2013 documentary television film directed by Louise Hooper, executive produced by Lucas Ochoa and produced by Caroline Page.  It was aired for the first time on 27 July 2013 on Channel 4, in conjunction with Pulse Films and One Little Indian Records.  Partly filmed at the Natural History Museum in London, the documentary features an encounter between Icelandic singer-songwriter and musician Björk and English broadcaster and naturalist David Attenborough.  They discuss the nature of music and the intersection between music, nature and technology.  It also follows the singer as she prepares for her Biophilia Tour, along with rehearsal and studio recording.

Stonemilker | "Stonemilker" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer/songwriter Björk for her eighth studio album, "Vulnicura", in 2015.  It was written and produced by Björk with beats by Venezuelan musician Arca.  "Stonemilker" is "Vulnicura"' s opening track, and the first of a six-part narrative that details the devastating end of Björk's relationship to American contemporary artist Matthew Barney.  Björk wrote the lyrics on the same Icelandic beach where the innovative 360-degree music video was later shot.

List of songs recorded by Björk | Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.  She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.  She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.  Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.
DJ Layla | DJ Layla, born in Chișinău, Moldavian SSR, Soviet Union) is a Moldovan DJ, record producer, singer and songwriter.

Another Life (Afrojack and David Guetta song) | "Another Life" is a song by Dutch DJ and record producer Afrojack and French DJ and record producer David Guetta, featuring American singer-songwriter Ester Dean.  It was released on 28 April 2017 via Afrojack's label Wall Recordings.

Beneath with Me | "Beneath with Me" is a collaborative song by American DJ, record producer and remixer Kaskade and Canadian record producer and DJ deadmau5.  It features vocals from American singer-songwriter Skylar Grey.  This is the third collaborative single by Kaskade and Deadmau5, having previously worked together on "I Remember" and "Move for Me".
hop2_titles: List of songs recorded by Björk
Another Life (Afrojack and David Guetta song)
response.summary_2: Selmasongs (the soundtrack to Dancer in the Dark) is by Björk, who is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.
